title: Nueva Extremadura

means "New Extremadura" in Spanish, and originates from [Extremadura](/wiki/Extremadura "Extremadura"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain").

Arreola, Daniel D. (2002). "3". *[Tejano South Texas: A Mexican American Cultural Province](/wiki/Tejano_South_Texas:_A_Mexican_American_Cultural_Province "Tejano South Texas: A Mexican American Cultural Province")*. [University of Texas Press](/wiki/University_of_Texas_Press "University of Texas Press"). p. 27. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780292757189](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780292757189 "Special:BookSources/9780292757189"). "Nueva Extremadura, a region penetrated in the sixteenth century and claimed by rival provinces—Nuevo Leon to its east and Nueva Vizcaya to its south and west—was a vaguely defined territory even after it was declared a separate gobierno in 1687" - [Read online at](https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.7560/705104/html) [DeGruyter](/wiki/DeGruyter "DeGruyter"). From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Old name for two Spanish colonies, either in South America or the Rio Grande Valley

**Nueva Extremadura** means "New Extremadura" in Spanish, and originates from [Extremadura](/wiki/Extremadura "Extremadura"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain").

**Nueva Extremadura** could refer to

* a large jurisdiction in the north of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain"), bordered in the 17th century to its west and south by [Nueva Vizcaya](/wiki/Nueva_Vizcaya,_New_Spain "Nueva Vizcaya, New Spain"), by [Nuevo León](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Le%C3%B3n "New Kingdom of León") to its southeast; by Nuevo Santander to its east, and, to its northeast, by Louisiana. After 1722, its northeastern border was the [New Philippines](/wiki/New_Philippines "New Philippines"). Today many identify the state of Coahuila in Mexico with the former Nueva Extremadura, but the present state's borders are not the same. For example, Nueva Vizcaya occupied the southwest of present-day Coahuila; Nuevo León, the neighbor to the east, also had claims to eastern New Extremaduran lands;[[1]](#cite_note-1) the border with the New Philippines was at the [Medina river](/wiki/Medina_River "Medina River") (near present-day San Antonio, in the U.S. state of Texas).[[2]](#cite_note-2) From 1687, the territory's official name was *Provincia de San Francisco de Coahuila y Nueva Extremadura.*[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

:   Until about 1722 Nueva Extremadura covered areas north of the Nueces River east of the Medina River. In 1722 [New Philippines](/wiki/New_Philippines "New Philippines") was established east of the Medina River. Effective Spanish control in this region had only been established in about 1722 and only actually existed from about 1718 and mainly in the general vicinity of San Antonio. So Nueva Extremadura did include some areas in what is now Texas.

* the name originally given to [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") by [Pedro de Valdivia](/wiki/Pedro_de_Valdivia "Pedro de Valdivia"). Its capital was then known as [Santiago de Nueva Extremadura](/wiki/Santiago_de_Nueva_Extremadura "Santiago de Nueva Extremadura").
